---
title: "False Edge in Prediction Markets"
subtitle: "Portability Lessons from Polymarket and Kalshi"
author: "Owen Brown"
date: 2026-03-14
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    css: assets/portfolio.css
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=1in
  docx: default
abstract: |
  **Question.** What survives when prediction-market edge claims are forced through the same
  leakage review, venue portability testing, and execution-realism analysis that serious
  quantitative operations already apply?

  **Approach.** I developed strategies against Polymarket data, subjected them to internal
  leakage audit, tested portability across 135 configurations on Kalshi, and built cost-aware
  selection and provenance infrastructure for the surviving methodology.

  **Main finding.** The primary backtest was invalidated by its own audit due to complete data
  leakage. The strategy did not port to Kalshi under any tested configuration. What remained
  was not alpha but engineering discipline: fee-adjusted selection logic, cross-venue provenance,
  and the willingness to kill results that could not survive review.

  **Why it matters.** This is a case study in how to handle results that look good but are not
  defensible, which is the situation that quant teams, trading desks, and research organizations
  face regularly.
---

## Research Problem

### Why prediction markets were a useful stress test

Prediction markets offered an accessible testing ground for quantitative methodology.
They are liquid enough to construct backtests, structured enough to formalize strategies,
and small enough in economic consequence that the real cost of failure is time rather
than capital. The research question was never whether prediction markets are profitable.
It was whether the process of building, testing, invalidating, and rebuilding strategies
against these markets could produce defensible methodology.

The work spanned several projects across Polymarket and Kalshi: a backtest-first strategy
development effort, a venue portability study, a fee-aware selection and monitoring system,
and a cross-venue provenance pipeline. Each project had its own audit trail. This page
reports what those audits actually found.

The thesis is direct: most of the claimed edge collapsed under review. What survived was
the review process itself.


## False Edge: What Failed Under Review

### Leakage invalidation

The primary Polymarket strategy produced results that appeared exceptional on first
inspection. Those results were subsequently invalidated by the project's own
data-leakage audit.[^1] The audit found complete overlap between training and test
data: the strategy had been optimized on the same observations it was evaluated
against. That made the reported backtest metrics meaningless.

The audit classified the leakage as severe and recommended immediately halting all
trading activity.[^1] This was not a subtle statistical concern. It was a
methodological failure in which training and evaluation used identical records,
eliminating any basis for generalization.

### The audit's own verdict

The project's overall quant-integrity status was recorded as a fail.[^2] A
substantial fraction of the project's quantitative claims were flagged as either
invalidated or of unknown status by the audit pipeline.[^2] The audit infrastructure
itself, not the original backtest, was what produced a defensible conclusion.

The lesson is not that backtesting is unreliable. The lesson is that an internal
review process caught a failure that would otherwise have reached production. That
review process is the defensible artifact from this work.


## Why Portability Failed

### Polymarket to Kalshi

After the primary backtest was invalidated, a separate research effort tested whether
the underlying strategy thesis could port to a different venue. The study analyzed
107,427 resolved Kalshi markets across 135 distinct strategy configurations.[^3]

The result was unambiguous: zero configurations produced positive edge. All showed
losses.[^3]

### Structural pricing difference

The core reason was structural.[^4] On Polymarket, buying NO contracts at low
prices creates an asymmetric payoff: a small cost for a large potential profit if
the event does not occur. On Kalshi, the pricing is symmetric. When YES trades at
five cents, the corresponding NO position costs ninety-five cents, not five. That
eliminates the asymmetry that made the Polymarket thesis attractive in the first
place.

Kalshi markets were efficiently priced at all tested levels: actual win rates
closely tracked the implied probabilities defined by contract prices, leaving no
systematic mispricing to exploit.[^4]

### Data limitations compounded the finding

The portability study carried an additional structural limitation.[^5] More than
99% of the resolved Kalshi markets in the dataset were sports or esports. The
categories where Polymarket strategies showed preliminary promise (politics,
weather, macroeconomic events) were nearly absent from the Kalshi data. No
historical price snapshots were available for time-to-close analysis, and no
multi-year date range existed for proper train/test separation.

That means the portability failure is real but not fully generalizable. The
structural pricing asymmetry finding holds regardless of category. But the
category-specific question remains open due to data coverage.


## What Survived After Edge Removal

### Methods, not returns

Once the backtest was invalidated and portability failed, what remained was not
performance but process. Three pieces of methodology survived review.

**Fee-adjusted selection logic.** The selection system (Project Haven) was
designed as Phase 1 infrastructure: market monitoring and candidate ranking with
no order placement, no position tracking, and no execution.[^6] The system
computed break-even probabilities, fee-adjusted expected values, and slippage
estimates for each ranked candidate. It imposed theme caps and per-underlying
diversification constraints to prevent hidden concentration.[^6]

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Fee math | f = 0.07 x P x (1 - P); break-even q* = (P + f) / (1 - 2f) |
| EV computation | q(1 - P - f) + (1 - q)(-P - f) with calibration margin |
| Slippage validation | Depth requirement at target size, max 1 cent impact |
| Diversification | Theme caps, per-underlying caps, max 60% single category |

**Explicit no-trading scope.** The selection system never claimed to trade. This is
a design choice that matters because the most common source of false confidence in
backtested strategies is the silent assumption that a tested signal is the same as a
deployed signal. By scoping the system as selection-only, the project avoided
confusing research output with trading output.

**Known-defect transparency.** Three critical bugs were identified in the selection
system at time of audit: price band filtering was not applied in watchlist mode,
theme caps were not enforced, and prices were storing as NULL in the database.[^7]
These defects were documented and tracked, not hidden. A system that is reviewed and
found defective under audit is more honest than one that ships without review.


## Why Provenance and Matching Mattered

### Cross-venue infrastructure

Cross-venue analysis requires knowing that two markets on different platforms refer
to the same underlying event. The prediction-market-logger project addressed this
with a two-stage pipeline: rule-based candidate detection followed by verification
using a language model.[^8]

### Cost-controlled verification

The design was cost-driven. A full scan of both venues at the rule-based stage was
free. Model-based verification was applied only to matched pairs, keeping per-scan
costs to fractions of a cent. Monthly costs at hourly cadence remained in the single
digits.[^8]

This is a small detail that reveals a larger principle. Data provenance is an
operational concern, not a theoretical one. If matching is unreliable, cross-venue
analysis is meaningless. If matching is too expensive, it will not run at the cadence
that research requires. The pipeline architecture reflected both constraints
explicitly.


## What This Page Does Not Claim

This project does not claim:

- profitable trading performance on any venue
- validated edge in any strategy configuration
- live or paper-traded results of any kind
- that the selection system was free of defects
- that the portability study had sufficient category coverage for a definitive conclusion
- that prediction-market strategies have no value in principle

The position is narrower: these specific strategies, under these specific review
conditions, did not survive. What survived was the methodology used to reach that
conclusion.


## Transfer to Adjacent Domains

The engineering patterns in this work have structural parallels in energy markets, grid
operations, and other constrained-optimization domains. Three brief examples, offered
as analogies rather than performance claims:

**Fee-adjusted candidate selection** parallels congestion routing. In both cases, the
question is not whether an opportunity exists in isolation but whether it clears costs,
constraints, and execution friction simultaneously.

**Correlation caps** in the selection system serve the same function as concentration
limits on a power book: preventing a portfolio that looks diversified from silently
becoming a single directional bet.

**Data provenance infrastructure** for cross-venue matching parallels the data pipeline
integrity requirements in ISO ingest systems, where a mismatched source identifier can
propagate silently through downstream analytics.

These are structural parallels. The prediction-market work does not prove competence in
energy markets. It demonstrates transferable engineering discipline applied to a
different asset class.


## Source Basis and Limitations

### Sources

All claims in this page are drawn from governance and audit artifacts produced by a
portfolio-audit pipeline. Style seeds (existing portfolio pages and published Kalshi
Research papers) were used for section shape and voice calibration only and are not
cited as factual evidence.

| Source | Trust Tier | Usage |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| polymarket-trader audit artifacts | T1 (project evidence) | Failure/invalidation only |
| kalshi-research audit artifacts | T1 (project evidence) | Portability failure and data limitations |
| kalshi-favorites-selector audit artifacts | T1 (project evidence) | Methods/system design; no-trading scope |
| prediction-market-logger audit artifacts | T1 (project evidence) | Provenance/matching/cost-control |
| Portfolio style seeds | T4 (style-only) | Section shape and voice; not factual evidence |
| market-design-liquidity.html | T5 (blocked) | Structural patterns only; no claims extracted |

### Limitations

The following limitations constrain the conclusions of this page:

1. **No validated out-of-sample performance** exists for any strategy on any venue.
   The primary backtest was invalidated. The portability study found no positive edge.
2. **Category bias in the Kalshi study.** More than 99% of resolved Kalshi markets
   were sports or esports. Categories of potential interest (politics, weather, macro)
   were absent, leaving the portability question partially open.
3. **Selection system defects.** Project Haven had three critical bugs at audit time
   and was never deployed for trading or paper trading.
4. **Matching pipeline not production-validated.** The cross-venue matching system
   was designed with cost controls but was not validated against a production-grade
   reconciliation standard.
5. **This page does not constitute investment advice,** trading recommendations, or
   claims of alpha generation.


## Endnotes

[^1]: polymarket-trader/quant-integrity-memo.md. The audit found "SEVERE DATA
LEAKAGE DETECTED" with "100% data overlap = Complete data leakage" and recommended
"STOP ALL TRADING IMMEDIATELY." The strategy was described as "trained and tested
on the exact same data, making the results meaningless."

[^2]: polymarket-trader/decision-memo.md (decision class: internal-only, total
score: 60, quant status: fail) and polymarket-trader/metric-audit.json
(quant_claim_count: 358, invalidated_count: 17, unknown_count: 29, status: fail).

[^3]: kalshi-research/writing-handoff.md. "Comprehensive analysis of 107,427
resolved Kalshi markets and testing 135 strategy configurations: 0% of
configurations showed positive edge. 0% achieved statistical significance.
All configurations showed massive losses."

[^4]: kalshi-research/writing-handoff.md, section 2.2 and 4.1. "On Kalshi: When
YES is at 5c, NO is at 95c (not 5c). Buy NO at 95c = Bet event WON'T happen.
Cost: 95c, Payout if correct: $1 (5c profit). Breakeven: 95% win rate." And:
"Markets are efficiently priced at all price levels. The actual win rate closely
matches the implied probability (breakeven), leaving no exploitable edge."

[^5]: kalshi-research/writing-handoff.md, section 1.4. "Category Bias: 99%+ of
data is sports/esports. Missing Categories: No politics, weather, or
macro-economic markets. Time Range: Only late 2025 data. Price Data: Only
available, not entry price at time of trade."

[^6]: kalshi-favorites-selector/writing-handoff.md. Phase 1 scope: "data
ingestion + selection only: Does NOT place trades, Does NOT track positions,
Does NOT execute orders." Fee calculation, break-even probability, EV
computation, slippage validation, and diversity constraints documented in
specification.

[^7]: kalshi-favorites-selector/writing-handoff.md, Critical Bugs section. Bug
#1: "No Price Band Filtering in Watchlist Mode" (CRITICAL). Bug #2: "Theme Caps
Not Working" (CRITICAL). Bug #3: "Prices Storing as NULL" (CRITICAL).

[^8]: prediction-market-logger/writing-handoff.md. "Rule-based detector finds
candidates (free, current system). LLM verifies only matched pairs (~50-100
pairs). Cost: ~$0.005/scan, $3.60/month at hourly checks."
